title: Correctly Freeing Memory in a Linked List

statement.markdown: Given a linked list head node containing an integer, and a function to delete the nth node from the end of the list. Implement the function. However, do not use a stack or a queue. Your implementation must free the memory of the deleted node.
statement.inputFormat: The input will be a linked list of integers. The first line will be the integer number of nodes, followed by each node's integer value. The second line will be an integer n, the node to delete from the end.
statement.outputFormat: If successful, the modified linked list.
statement.notes: Freeing the memory of the deleted node is key

constraints.description: Node to delete from the end

examples.explanation: n is 2, which is the third node from the end
n is 2, which is the fourth node from the end

hints.text: Keep track of two pointers, both initialized to the head of the list.
When deleting a node, consider freeing the memory of the deleted node.

editorial.title: Linked List Editor
editorial.intuition: To solve this problem, we can use two pointers to keep track of the current node and the node before the current node.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach would be to convert the linked list to a list and delete the nth to last element.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can solve this problem in O(n) time complexity by keeping track of two pointers.

functionSignature.java: public ListNode removeNthFromEnd(ListNode head, int n);
functionSignature.python: def removeNthFromEnd(self, head: ListNode, n: int) -> ListNode:
